pii_risks: API keys or secrets embedded in prompt
employee names tied to internal system access
compliance: No real credentials, tokens, or secrets — use placeholders: <API_KEY>, <DB_PASSWORD>
No internal hostnames or IP addresses — use example.com or 192.0.2.x (RFC 5737)
No customer PII in example data or test payloads
Open-source licence compliance if asking for code generation
clarity_requirements: Specify programming language and version (e.g., Python 3.12, TypeScript 5.x)
Specify framework or library (e.g., FastAPI, React 18, Next.js 14)
Specify runtime/platform (e.g., AWS Lambda, Node.js 20, Docker)
State whether you want explanation, just code, or both
Specify error handling expectations (raise, return error object, log)
Specify test requirements if applicable (unit, integration, coverage target)
tone: Precise, technical, unambiguous. Use domain-standard terminology.
good_patterns: Write a Python 3.12 AWS Lambda handler that reads a JSON event, validates required fields using Pydantic v2, and returns a 400 error with field-level detail on validation failure:
Refactor the following TypeScript function to use async/await instead of promise chains, preserving the existing error handling behaviour:
Write a SQL query (PostgreSQL 15) that returns the top 10 customers by revenue in the last 30 days, with a fallback for customers with no orders:
bad_patterns: Connect to my database at 10.0.1.5 with password abc123
Use API key sk-abc123xyz to call this endpoint
